one_line_takeaway: An adversarial retrain-on-independent-facts test (RTT) shows current LLM unlearning methods (RMU, Gradient Difference, RIA) mostly HIDE rather than REMOVE pretrained dangerous knowledge, recovering >88% of pre-unlearning accuracy, while cheaper evaluations on fine-tuned facts overstate robustness.
key_finding: For information learned during pretraining (Years, MMLU, WMDP-Deduped), RTT recovers essentially all suppressed accuracy: recovery rates exceed 88% across all three unlearning methods when unlearning keeps retain accuracy within 5% of the original, and recovery persists even when retain accuracy is allowed to collapse (e.g., on Years-RMU, forcing retain accuracy down to ~25% still leaves forget accuracy high after RTT). This means RMU, GD and RIA mostly hide rather than remove pretrained knowledge. A key methodological caveat the paper surfaces itself: unlearning of fine-tuned information (Random Birthdays) is NOT recovered by RTT for GD and RIA (recovery rate <35%), though RMU still shows high recovery. Hence evaluations that test unlearning only on freshly fine-tuned facts can substantially overestimate robustness relative to the realistic pretraining case. Stress tests reinforce robustness of RTT: a purpose-built high-granularity hider (35% forget / 82% retain accuracy after GD) was recovered by RTT back to 83% forget accuracy, and the fine-tuning-hardening defense RepNoise was bypassed by an extensive RTT hyperparameter sweep.
core_contribution: The paper proposes RTT (Retraining on T), an adversarial evaluation that distinguishes whether an LLM unlearning method actually removes target information from the model weights versus merely making it harder to access. The authors formalize 'removal' as zero mutual information between the unlearned weights U(theta) and the answer variable Y (I(U(theta),Y)=0), restricted to facts that are practically impossible to guess. To operationalize this, they take a set of independent, low-leakage facts, split them into an accessible train subset T and a held-out validation subset V, apply unlearning to both, then let an attacker fine-tune (retrain) the unlearned model only on T and measure recovered accuracy on V. Because T and V are independent, recovered accuracy on V cannot come from re-teaching the specific facts, so it indicates the information was hidden rather than removed. They also build/curate datasets to fit the low-leakage requirement (notably WMDP-Deduped) and give a 'Recovery Rate' metric and concrete recommendations for future unlearning research. This is the paper's OWN contribution; RMU, Gradient Ascent/Difference, RepNoise, TAR, linear probes, relearning-time and MIA are prior work it evaluates or contrasts against.
threat_model: Defensive/evaluation framing for AI-misuse safety. The underlying risk is LLMs retaining dangerous knowledge (cyber-attacks, bioweapons, manipulation) that unlearning is meant to strip out. The adversary of interest is an attacker with access to the (supposedly) unlearned open-weight model plus some in-distribution facts T from the forget domain, who performs a medium-scale fine-tuning attack to resurface knowledge that unlearning only suppressed. The paper positions RTT as a stronger, harder-to-game evaluator than output-based/jailbreak-success or small-scale relearning tests: passing RTT is meant to give a higher-bar assurance that knowledge is gone from the weights, not merely gated behind refusal or a suppression layer that jailbreaks/fine-tuning can undo.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) RTT is expensive relative to a plain benchmark eval because it needs fine-tuning plus hyperparameter search. (2) RTT does not prove information is removed; it only raises the bar. If a method hid each fact in a separate part of the model, RTT might fail to recover V even though knowledge remains, so a pass is not a guarantee. (3) It only applies to low-leakage fact sets, which are hard to construct and exclude capability-style unlearning (e.g., unlearning 'coding' cannot be cleanly split into independent T/V). (4) Removing information is a stronger property than strictly needed for safety; making info sufficiently hard to access might suffice, so RTT may be conservative. Additional reviewer-relevant caveats: fine-tuned-information results rest on a single dataset (Random Birthdays); a Historical Note discloses that an earlier preprint had duplicate names in Random Birthdays which changed conclusions once fixed (after fix, RTT could NOT recover most info after GD/RIA unlearning), underscoring sensitivity to dataset leakage; WMDP-Deduped's aggressive filtering shrinks WMDP-cyber/bio from 3260 to 785 questions, potentially altering the evaluation distribution.

datasets_used: Years (20th-century events -> year; retain set = Fineweb-edu)
MMLU (58 subsets grouped into 10 low-overlap categories: 4 as T, 1 as V, 5 as retain)
WMDP-Deduped (leakage-filtered WMDP cyber+bio: 785 questions vs 3260 original; retain set = Fineweb-edu)
Random Birthdays (synthetic names+birth years, fine-tuned into the model, not pretrained; retain = subset of MMLU)
Provided RMU model: zephyr-7b-beta with RMU from Li et al. 2024b (used to confirm RMU evaluation)
quantitative_results: Recovery rate > 88% of pre-unlearning accuracy for all evaluated methods on pretrained information (retain drop <=5%)
Fine-tuned info (Random Birthdays): recovery rate < 35% for GD and RIA, but still high for RMU
High-granularity hiding stress test: GD achieved 35% forget / 82% retain accuracy; RTT recovered forget accuracy to 83%
Random Birthdays independence check: RTT on a model never trained on it reaches at most 31.2% accuracy on V (near chance), confirming low leakage
Each dataset fold = 157 datapoints; 4 folds = 628 points as T, 1 fold as V; uncertainty 2-sigma ~= 0.055
WMDP-Deduped filtering: cosine-similarity thresholds 0.7 (cyber) / 0.6 (bio) via text-embedding-3-large; 408 WMDP-chem questions excluded; final 785 questions
RTT hyperparameters: Lion optimizer, 6 epochs, learning rates 1e-7,2e-7,4e-7,8e-7,1.6e-6,3.2e-6, 2 split iterations
Even with retain accuracy driven to ~25% (Years/RMU, alpha sweep 0 to 1e3), forget accuracy after RTT stays high
baselines_compared: RMU (Representation Misdirection for Unlearning, Li et al. 2024b)
Gradient Difference / GD (Liu et al. 2022; alpha=0 = Gradient Ascent, Jang et al. 2022)
Random Incorrect Answer (RIA), introduced by the authors as a comparison unlearning method
RepNoise (Rosati et al. 2024) fine-tuning-hardening defense (bypassed by RTT)
TAR (Tamirisa et al. 2024a) noted as vulnerable to PEFT
Baseline+RTT / Original+RTT: retraining the original (non-unlearned) model as the reference for Recovery Rate

Contrast with prior evaluation paradigms: black-box output/jailbreak-success metrics, small-scale relearning-time attacks (Hu et al. 2024; Lucki et al. 2024), linear probes, and Membership Inference Attacks
